1.description: Career (Partnership) Aspirations (by Gender) Notes . The figure plots, by gender, the responses to the question: “How strongly do you aspire to attain an equity partner position within your firm?” measured in 2007. This is measured on a 10-point Likert scale (from 1 (not at all) to 10 (very high)). We restrict the data to individuals who are observed billing at least one hour in our data. The figure compares the responses for men and women.

2.description: Career Aspirations and Actual Promotion Note . The figure plots, by career aspiration groups (low (1), medium(2), high (3)), measured in 2007, the proportion of individuals who were promoted to partner in 2012.

3.description: Aspirations and Effort Notes . The figure plots, by career aspiration groups (low (1), medium(2), high (3)), the following. (a) We plot the proportion of individuals who are promoted to partner in 2012. (b) We plot the number of annual hours worked (expressed in thousands of hours), measured in 2007. (c) We plot the number of annual hours billed (expressed in thousands of hours), measured in 2007. (d) We plot the probability of changing firms within the first five years, measured in 2007. We restrict the data to individuals who are observed billing at least one hour over the sample period.

4.description: Aspirations and Expectations Notes . The figure plots, by career aspiration groups (low (1), medium(2), high(3)), the expectations of being promoted to partner (“How would you rate your chances, as a percentage ranging from zero to 100, of attaining each of the following positions in your firm?”). We bin the responses into deciles, measured in 2007. We restrict the data to individuals who are observed billing at least one hour over the sample period.
